interview_name: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman

main_summary.summary: Joint oral history with David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman, classmates at Chicago Theological Seminary (CTS) who traveled to Selma, Alabama in March 1965 following Jesse Jackson's convocation appeal at the seminary in the days after Bloody Sunday. Satoko Ito Ackerman recounts her path from Kobe College in Nishinomiya, Japan to Yankton College in South Dakota (1959) through a Congregational Church scholarship, then to CTS for graduate work. David Ackerman recounts growing up in Mt. Carroll, Illinois, attending Knox College in Galesburg, and entering CTS after his brother's suicide during Ranger training at Fort Benning following the military's discovery that he was gay. Both describe Jesse Jackson as a CTS classmate, the convocation at which he commandeered the meeting to issue Dr. King's call for ministers, the twelve-person two-van caravan to Selma, sheltering with Black families in Selma, Dr. King's rally at Brown Chapel A.M.E. Church, the news of Jim Reeb's beating, the Thursday line-of-march arranged with Black women at the center, the all-night vigil after Jim Clark blocked the march at the Edmund Pettus Bridge approach, and the sunrise moment when troopers removed their helmets during the national anthem. The interview concludes with the Ackermans' move to Silver Spring, Maryland and a 1970s incident in which a white neighbor forbade his daughters from playing with a Black neighbor child.
key_themes: Chicago Theological Seminary and Jesse Jackson's convocation call
Selma to Montgomery voting rights campaign (March 1965)
Interfaith and interracial seminary response to Dr. King's appeal
Black community hospitality and protective vigil for visiting marchers
Continuing racial discrimination in Northern suburbs (Silver Spring, Maryland)
main_summary.historical_significance: The Ackermans' account documents the white seminary student response to Dr. King's 'all people of good will' call following Bloody Sunday, with first-person detail on how Jesse Jackson personally mobilized Chicago Theological Seminary students, the moral wrestling of fellow students (including a Texas classmate disowned by his family for considering the trip), and the racial geography of Selma observed by first-time visitors. Satoko Ito Ackerman's perspective as a Japanese-born student adds a non-Black, non-white view of the line-of-march arrangement (Black women centered, then Black men, then white women, then white men on the outside) and the protective shelter provided by Black host families who maintained overnight guard outside their homes. The interview preserves a specific recollection of Reverend Ralph Abernathy leading the all-night vigil at the bridge approach in Dr. King's absence, of Sheriff Jim Clark, and of the morning sunrise moment when Alabama troopers removed their helmets during the singing of the national anthem. David Ackerman's path from seminary to law school and the National Council of Churches' Washington office anti-poverty advocacy work, alongside the 1970s Silver Spring incident, situates Selma within a continuing civil rights trajectory rather than as a discrete event.

chapters.title: Satoko Ito's Path From Kobe to Yankton
chapters.summary: Satoko Ito Ackerman recounts being raised in the Osaka area and attending Kobe College in Nishinomiya, a women's mission school where she was introduced to English and Christianity. A missionary, Angie Crew, arranged a scholarship through the South Dakota Conference of Congregational Churches, and she arrived at Yankton College in August 1959, later returning through the Toko Fund for seminary graduate work.

chapters.title: David Ackerman's Mt. Carroll Childhood and Brother's Death
chapters.summary: David Ackerman describes growing up in Mt. Carroll, Illinois, a farming town of two thousand, and majoring in history and political science at Knox College in Galesburg. During his senior year his brother died by suicide after the military discovered he was gay during Ranger training at Fort Benning and threatened him with dishonorable discharge, which led David to Chicago Theological Seminary.

chapters.title: Satoko's First Awareness of Race
chapters.summary: Satoko recalls that race only entered her consciousness with the idea of going to Selma, after she saw the film of Bloody Sunday on television and heard Jesse Jackson speak. She qualifies this by remembering that African students at Yankton College could not get haircuts in town, an incident that outraged her at the time but soon faded from her thoughts.

chapters.title: Mt. Carroll, Grief, and Meeting in Chicago
chapters.summary: David describes Mt. Carroll as a lily-white community with an air of tolerance rarely put to the test, recalling one Black family that was graciously received while he was in high school. He notes that his brother's death struck him as personal grief rather than political tragedy at the time, and that he and Satoko met and fell in love at the seminary the year before Selma.
related_events: David and Satoko meet at Chicago Theological Seminary

chapters.title: Seminary at Hyde Park and Woodlawn
chapters.summary: David explains that Chicago Theological Seminary sits on the University of Chicago campus in Hyde Park, just across the Midway from Woodlawn, a predominantly Black and economically depressed community. Clinical activities took students into the Black parts of Chicago, where he was struck by the extent of poverty and the difference between the North Side and South Side.
related_events: Seminary clinical work in Black Chicago

chapters.title: From the Early Sixties to Jesse Jackson's Class
chapters.summary: David surveys the civil rights decade he lived through at a distance, from Birmingham and the March on Washington to the Montgomery Bus Boycott, and his resonance with Kennedy's call to public service. He notes that Selma became personal almost by accident because Jesse Jackson was part of his seminary class, an accomplished preacher who had come to CTS for formal training, and that Bloody Sunday occurred on March 7, 1965 as the SCLC made Selma a focal point for voting rights legislation.

chapters.part: The Seminary and the Call to Selma
chapters.title: Jesse Jackson Commandeers the Convocation
chapters.summary: David recounts the seminary's weekly convocation of faculty and students at which Jesse Jackson, in his powerful style, commandeered the meeting and made Selma a personal moral issue, relaying Dr. King's call for all people of good will and asking directly who would go with him. Satoko describes feeling immediately called to go despite being a foreign student and a woman, while David grew upset because he felt he would have to take care of her.

chapters.part: The Seminary and the Call to Selma
chapters.title: Jesse Jackson, From Self-Important to Leader
chapters.summary: After a recording break, both Ackermans reflect on Jesse Jackson as a young classmate they barely knew personally and initially found self-important and marked by vanity. Each recalls that on the night of the convocation he became a genuine leader, speaking with such passion, sincerity, and integrity that it was impossible not to respond.

chapters.part: The Seminary and the Call to Selma
chapters.title: Seminary Discussions and the Protector Impulse
chapters.summary: Asked whether race and gender were discussed in seminary training, both Ackermans say they recall little beyond a course on the sociology of Chicago and its ecological barriers. David explains that his concern about Satoko joining the trip was driven by the prospect of violence and his playing the traditional male role of protector, which Satoko has continuously educated him was neither needed nor desired.

chapters.part: The Seminary and the Call to Selma
chapters.title: Two Vans, Moral Wrestling, and the Texas Classmate
chapters.summary: David recounts that twelve students traveled in two vans with Illinois plates, taking turns driving roughly six hundred and fifty miles of two-lane road to Selma. He frames the challenge as a moral wrestling placed before the whole community, remembering most vividly a close friend from Amarillo, Texas who agonized over the trip and ultimately stayed behind because his parents would disown him and cut off his seminary funding.
related_events: Twelve seminarians decide to go to Selma

chapters.part: The Seminary and the Call to Selma
chapters.title: Whether to Tell Their Parents
chapters.summary: Asked whether they consulted their parents before leaving, Satoko says she had been making decisions independently since coming to the States and that her parents may never have known. She contrasts her own decision with the Texas friend's far harder struggle, while David says his parents might have been supportive but he was not willing to test it, and his scholarship meant no funding was at risk.

chapters.part: The Seminary and the Call to Selma
chapters.title: Dr. Schomer Says Do Not Go
chapters.summary: The Ackermans recall that no one at the seminary called the trip a bad idea, since the students accepted it as a moral challenge fitting people studying theology and ethics. Seminary president Dr. Howard Schomer told them not to go, citing upcoming exams and the students' place being at the seminary, even though he was going himself out of concern that violence could be an issue.

chapters.part: The Seminary and the Call to Selma
chapters.title: Schomer's Sincerity and the Seminary's Makeup
chapters.summary: Both Ackermans say they did not take Dr. Schomer's warning to heart, feeling they were needed in Selma, and neither recalls anyone taking his words seriously. Asked about the seminary's composition, they estimate a small nonwhite enrollment, perhaps ten to fifteen percent at most with only three or four African American students, out of a total of roughly seventy or eighty people.

chapters.title: The Gas Station Threat in Alabama
chapters.summary: Once across the state line into Alabama, the group stopped at a gas station where Satoko and her friend Ginny walked around, oblivious to danger. Jesse Jackson suddenly shouted for everyone to get in the car after white men loitering at the station went to a payphone to make a call, and the group took off fast with a car following them for some distance before it turned away.

chapters.title: Ministerial Collars and Arrival
chapters.summary: Shortly after the pursuit the group stopped and put on ministerial collars they had brought along, though none were ordained, for whatever protective help they might provide. David recalls leaving Chicago on Tuesday mid-afternoon and arriving in Selma early morning or midday on Wednesday after driving straight through the night.

chapters.title: The Stark Racial Geography of Selma
chapters.summary: David and Satoko describe the stark difference between the white and Black sides of Selma. Crossing into the Black side, the streets were no longer paved, there were no sidewalks or streetlights, the houses looked shabby, and there was simply no evidence of public services.

chapters.title: Brown Chapel Rally and the Power of Singing
chapters.summary: The group reached Brown Chapel A.M.E. Church, where people white and Black milled around and a rally built toward hearing from Dr. King, the church overflowing to the rafters. Satoko describes encountering powerful civil rights singing for the first time, more powerful than anything she had heard.

chapters.title: The News of Jim Reeb's Beating
chapters.summary: At Brown Chapel the Ackermans heard about Jim Reeb, a Unitarian minister who had come quickly in response to Dr. King's call. He had walked into the white part of the community for dinner on Tuesday night and, with one or two companions, had been brutally beaten and was lying in critical condition at the hospital.

chapters.title: Welcomed and Fed by the Community
chapters.summary: Satoko describes the welcome at the church as overwhelming, with community members thanking the visitors profusely and making her feel more appreciated than she ever had been. They kept bringing food to tables that were never empty, and it was clear the visitors were the community's guests.

chapters.title: The Schoolteachers Who Stood Guard
chapters.summary: Satoko and Ginny were told by women at the church to come with two schoolteachers who had built a new home on the outskirts of Selma. The hosts made them comfortable and left, and in the morning the family revealed they had been so worried that someone stayed guard outside all night, which moved Satoko deeply since she had slept without knowing.

chapters.title: David's Lodging and Stories of Injustice
chapters.summary: David stayed in what seemed like public housing in the Black community, where people were incredibly gracious in feeding and housing the visitors. That night he heard for the first time stories of the rapes and periodic lynchings of young men in Selma, and got the sense that no justice system operated there.

chapters.title: The Climate of Concern for Safety
chapters.summary: Asked about concern for personal safety, David says that oddly he did not feel endangered that first night, even though he and Ginny had walked to the edge where the Black and white communities met and faced vile accusations from white youth across the line. He had not yet heard about Jim Reeb and was somehow blocking out what had happened on Bloody Sunday.

chapters.title: Dr. King Speaks at Brown Chapel
chapters.summary: Returning to the rally, Satoko recalls her first real hearing of Dr. King, who spoke powerfully and thanked those whose conscience had brought them to Selma. He explained that a court appearance would keep him from leading the march in body though he would be with them in spirit, and that Reverend Ralph Abernathy would lead in his place.

chapters.title: Forming the Thursday Line of March
chapters.summary: David recalls his understanding that an attempted Tuesday march had reached only the foot of the Pettus Bridge, and that another march was set for Thursday. Satoko describes how the marchers lined up on a dirt road outside the church in deliberate order, Black women in the middle, Black men flanking them, then white women, then white men on the outside, fifteen to twenty abreast in their Sunday best.
related_events: Thursday march toward Edmund Pettus Bridge

chapters.title: Locked Arms and the Troopers Appear
chapters.summary: Satoko describes how locking arms at dusk sent something like electricity through her body, the most powerful thing she had ever experienced, even as she felt afraid. As the marchers stood, Alabama troopers with reflective sunglasses, helmets, and batons suddenly surrounded the formation, looking fierce, yet she felt she could do anything with this group, both protector and protected.
related_events: Thursday march toward Edmund Pettus Bridge

chapters.title: Jim Clark Halts the March
chapters.summary: After three or four steps a booming voice from the head of the troopers, Sheriff Jim Clark of Dallas County, declared they could go no further. Reverend Abernathy said let us pray and the marchers knelt, beginning a night of vigil in which Satoko heard for the first time amazing life stories of what it was like to live in the South.
related_events: Thursday march stopped at the bridge approach

chapters.title: A Night of Testimony From Selma's People
chapters.summary: Satoko recounts the testimony shared during the vigil, of people who summoned great courage to register to vote only to be turned away on made-up reasons, and of friends' daughters who were raped and people abused in many ways. Despite a whole night of such stories, the people refused to give up and kept saying they were glad the visitors were there to help them win.
related_events: All-night vigil at the bridge approach

chapters.title: David's Fear and the Journalists' Lights
chapters.summary: David shares similar recollections with one difference, that as a white man he had the place of honor on the outside of the line of march and felt fear and vulnerability there, with Bloody Sunday vivid in his mind and troopers arrayed across the road. He emphasizes that the television networks kept their lights on all night, which he calls their guardian angel against what could have been another bloody scene.
related_events: All-night vigil at the bridge approach

chapters.title: Immense Respect for Reverend Abernathy
chapters.summary: David expresses immense respect for Dr. Abernathy, noting that as an SCLC leader Abernathy was a little bumbling and not the greatest organizer, but could pray as if he had a direct pipeline to God. Both agree it was Abernathy who kept the whole march together that night and nobody else.
related_events: All-night vigil at the bridge approach

chapters.title: The Helmets Come Off at Sunrise
chapters.summary: Toward early morning the sun came up and Abernathy told the marchers to rise and sing the national anthem. Satoko describes the troopers' quandary over whether to keep their helmets on, until everyone looked to a flustered Jim Clark, who finally removed his helmet and the others immediately followed, a moment that felt like a communication and a community beyond the marchers.

chapters.title: The Address to the Oppressor
chapters.summary: David calls the helmets-off moment a wonderful representation of what the nonviolent movement was about, since King and others addressed not only the oppressed but the oppressor, trying to call forth the better angels of our natures. The interviewer remarks on the many ironies embedded in the scene.

chapters.title: No Roller Coaster, Only the Vigil
chapters.summary: Asked whether the night brought a jumble of feeling or a crystallization, David says he does not remember any roller coaster of emotion. Once the marchers came to a halt after a few steps and were caught up in the all-night vigil, his sense of fear disappeared and the whole night became an uplifting spiritual experience.
related_events: All-night vigil at the bridge approach

chapters.title: A Community of Atonement
chapters.summary: Satoko frames the experience as a community of atonement, pronounced at-one-ment, in which disparate people locked arms and marched together toward justice and truth, a healing that brought together all the ills and produced new life. She explains the march became a paradigm and an Aha moment for her life, and that she wrote her seminary thesis afterward on atoning community in nursery school children.

chapters.title: Friday Breakfast With Jim Farmer
chapters.summary: David recalls that the experience carried through to Friday, though his memory becomes vague, with the twelve students and Jesse Jackson going out to breakfast. Jackson had arranged for Jim Farmer, head of the Congress of Racial Equality, to join them, a very dynamic man, and after the breakfast the group, including Jackson, left for home on an uneventful return to Chicago.

chapters.title: The Long View of Civil Rights
chapters.summary: Taking the long view, David reflects that the past half-century has been an extraordinary stretch for civil rights in this country, and not only for Black members of society. He traces the widening movement from there to women's liberation, to the disabled and handicapped, and finally to gay, lesbian, and transgender people.

chapters.title: Don't Ask Don't Tell Ends on Interview Day
chapters.summary: The interviewer notes for the record that they meet on the first day the U.S. military no longer bars gay servicemembers. Given what happened to his brother, David says he celebrates the day, calling the abolition of the Don't Ask Don't Tell rule too long in coming.
related_events: Repeal of Don't Ask Don't Tell

chapters.title: From Seminary to Law and Public Service
chapters.summary: David says what most stayed with him was the clear absence of a justice structure in Selma and the incredible disparity between the Black and white communities, which played a role in his dropping out of seminary to attend law school instead. Answering Kennedy's call to public service, he first worked for the Washington office of the National Council of Churches doing anti-poverty advocacy and then moved into congressional research, all underlain by what Dr. King stood for.
related_events: David Ackerman's transition to law and public service

chapters.title: A Black Family Moves to Bradford Road
chapters.summary: Satoko recounts that after moving to a suburb of Washington, D.C., she found racial discrimination was not finished. In the early 1970s on Bradford Road in their all-white community, a Black family with two children moved in across the street, and she delightedly introduced their little girl to five girls in the house behind them, who all had a good time together that first day.

chapters.title: Vera Turned Away
chapters.summary: The next day the little girl, Vera, returned crestfallen because the other children told her their father had said she could not come and play anymore. Satoko, angry both at having put Vera in that position and at the family's discrimination, marched out and gathered the girls together to ask why Vera could not play, and the oldest, around twelve or thirteen, said it was because she was Black.

chapters.title: Play With Anybody in Our Yard
chapters.summary: Satoko told the girls that while she knew they had to obey their father, when they came to play in the Ackermans' yard between the two houses they would have to play with anybody there, including Vera, and they agreed and did so a few times. She concludes that the experience showed her things were not finished and that vigilance is needed because prejudice is passed on in little ways that affect little children.

chapters.title: The Boulder Up the Mountain
chapters.summary: Satoko confirms the community was Silver Spring, Maryland, a northern suburb about ten miles from the White House in Montgomery County. Thanked for sharing their history, David offers a final thought that the battle goes on, pointing to drug laws used against the Black community and new attempts to limit voting rights, so that somehow that boulder keeps having to be pushed up the mountain.

loc_video.caption: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in Washington, D.C., 2011 September 20
